turns.text: Okay, the deadline is in three weeks.
And the final chapter is a complete mess.
My protagonist, Kaelen, needs to confront the Void Queen.
But every draft feels emotionally flat.
Like the stakes just vanish.
Maybe it's because Kaelen's motivation got muddy.
He started this journey to save his sister, Lyra.
But she's already been rescued two chapters ago.
So what's driving him now?
Pure revenge against the Queen?
That feels too simplistic for his character.
He's a healer, not a warrior at heart.
The magic system is based on emotional resonance, too.
So the climax needs an emotional core.
Maybe Lyra isn't as safe as he thought.
Or the Queen's corruption has a personal link to him.
Right, what if the Void Queen is his lost mother?
No, that's a cliché I wanted to avoid.
But a familial twist could work if done right.
Let me dig deeper into their backstories.
Kaelen’s mother vanished when he was seven.
The records say she died in a border skirmish.
But what if she was taken by the Void?
Not as its queen, but as its first victim.
Her essence is trapped within the corruption.
So Kaelen isn’t fighting to destroy the Queen.
He’s fighting to free her.
That’s a healer’s goal, not a warrior’s.
But does that raise the stakes enough?
Maybe the Queen’s defeat would erase his mother forever.
So he faces an impossible choice.
Save the realm or save her.
Lyra could be the key here.
She’s always been the pragmatic one.
What if she urges him to let their mother go?
That creates a brutal sibling conflict at the climax.
Yes, that has the emotional core I need.
But the magic system must reflect this.
His healing power relies on connection, not force.
So the confrontation becomes a desperate act of empathy.
But how does he free her without destroying everything?
The Void is consuming the land.
Maybe he has to offer a piece of his own essence.
A sacrifice that mirrors his mother's original capture.
That feels right for the magic system.
But what would that cost him?
His healing power, perhaps.
His ability to ever use magic again.
Lyra would hate that plan.
She'd see it as throwing his life away.
Perfect conflict.
Now, the Queen's physical form.
It should be a twisted reflection of his mother.
Familiar eyes in a monstrous face.
That visual gives me chills.
Okay, so the chapter opens with them facing her.
Kaelen realizes the truth immediately.
Lyra doesn't believe him at first.
The Queen attacks, forcing a reaction.
And Kaelen refuses to fight back.
He tries to reach her with a memory.
Maybe the lullaby she used to sing.
The Void recoils at the pure emotion.
Lyra sees it working and panics.
She thinks he's being manipulated.
She draws her sword to strike the Queen.
Kaelen has to shield his mother instead.
That's the moment of sacrifice.
He channels his healing magic into the lullaby.
It starts to unravel the corruption.
But the Void needs a new anchor.
It begins to latch onto him.
Lyra realizes what's happening too late.
She tries to pull him back.
His mother's form clears for a second.
She speaks his name.
Then she begs him to stop.
But he can't now.
The transfer is almost complete.
The cost is his connection to magic.
Lyra uses her own magic to sever the link.
She takes the corruption into herself instead.
No, that's too heroic for her character.
She'd do it to save him, though.
But it can't be a clean save.
Maybe she only partially succeeds.
The Void splits between them.
Kaelen loses his magic.
Lyra is left scarred but alive.
Their mother is freed, but fading.
She gets a final moment with them.
A quiet goodbye, not a grand speech.
Then the realm begins to heal.
The cost feels real and earned.
Kaelen is a healer without his gift.
Lyra carries a physical reminder.
Their relationship is changed, not broken.
That's the bittersweet ending I wanted.
The emotional core finally clicks.
I can write this chapter now.
But I need to figure out the exact moment of the severing.
Does she use a spell or is it more instinctual?
Instinct feels right for her.
A raw, desperate act of will.
She reaches into the flow of magic between them.
And she just... rips.
The backlash is immediate and violent.
It leaves a mark on her, like a burn or a crackle of dark energy under her skin.
Kaelen collapses, the magic gone from him like a snuffed candle.
The Void energy, now split, recoils and dissipates.
Their mother's form becomes clear, but translucent.
She's free, but anchored to this world by a thread.
She kneels, one hand on each of their heads.
No words, just a look of profound sorrow and love.
Then she fades into light motes.
The silence afterward is heavy.
Lyra helps Kaelen to his feet.
He tries to summon a healing light, but nothing happens.
The realization hits him then.
Lyra doesn't apologize. She just says, 'We're alive.'
He looks at his hands, then at her.
'What did you do?' he whispers.
'What I had to,' she says, her voice steady but tired.
The mark on her arm pulses with a dull, cold ache.
He reaches for it, but she pulls back.
'Don't. It's... unstable.'
They stand in the ruins of the ritual chamber.
The storm outside has calmed.
Dawn light filters through the broken ceiling.
'I can't feel it,' he says, a tremor in his voice.
'The magic. It's just... gone.'
Lyra nods. 'For both of us.'
That's the real cost. She severed her own connection too.
He stares at her, understanding dawning.
'You're mortal now.'
'We both are,' she corrects.
A strange, hollow laugh escapes him.
'All that power. All that legacy. Gone.'
'It was killing us,' she says simply.
She turns to leave the chamber, her steps heavy.
'Lyra, wait.'
She pauses at the doorway, not looking back.
'What now?' he asks, the emptiness inside him vast.
'We live,' she says, her voice barely a whisper.
He follows her out into the morning light.
The air is clean, sharp with the scent of rain and broken stone.
For the first time in centuries, he feels the cold.
Lyra wraps her arms around herself, shivering.
They stand on the mountainside, looking at the world below.
'No one will be coming for us,' she says after a long silence.
'The storm would have been seen for miles. They'll think we're dead.'
He nods. It's a clean break. A strange freedom.
'The mark,' he says, gesturing to her arm. 'Will it fade?'
She glances at it, the dark lines stark against her skin. 'I don't know.'
'It hurts you.'
'A reminder,' she says, and starts walking down the path.
He falls into step beside her, his body aching in new, mundane ways.
Every step is heavier. Every breath is a conscious effort.
'Where are we going?'
'Away from here. Somewhere we can rest.'
'There's a village at the base of the mountain. I saw the smoke from the tower.'
'We can't go there looking like this.'
He looks down at his torn, ash-stained robes.
'We'll find a stream first. Clean up.'
The path winds down through pine trees, the ground soft with needles.
The silence between them is thick, but not hostile.
'Do you think the core is truly gone?'
She stops walking, her eyes distant. 'I felt it die.'
'Then it's over.'
'For us. Not for the ones who come looking for its power.'
He hadn't considered that. The thought is a new weight.
'They'll find ruins. Scorch marks. Nothing to claim.'
'They'll find us, if we're not careful.'
She pushes a low-hanging branch aside, revealing a small, clear stream.
The water is icy. He kneels and cups some in his hands.
It washes away the grime, but not the fatigue.
Lyra sits on a rock, staring at her reflection.
The mark on her arm seems to pulse faintly in the dappled light.
'It's not fading,' she says, her voice flat.
'Maybe it needs time.'
'Or maybe it never will.'
She dips her hand into the water, watching the ripples distort her face.
'We should keep moving. Get to the village before dark.'
He stands, his knees protesting. 'What will we tell them?'
'Travelers. Caught in a rockslide. It's close enough to the truth.'
They follow the stream downhill, the sound of water a constant companion.
The trees begin to thin. The smell of woodsmoke grows stronger.
A wooden fence appears ahead, marking the village boundary.
They pause at the tree line, watching a few figures move between simple huts.
'Ready?' she asks, not looking at him.
He takes a deep breath. 'No. But let's go.'
They approach the gate, trying to look weary but not desperate.
An old man tending a garden looks up, his eyes narrowing.
'You two look like you've seen better days.'
'The mountain wasn't kind to us,' Lyra says, her voice carefully neutral.
The man nods slowly. 'We heard thunder up there last night. Strange lights.'
'Just a storm,' he says, the lie tasting like ash.
'Hmph. Well, the inn's that way. Tell Mara I sent you.'
They walk past him, feeling his gaze on their backs.
The village is quiet, but the air feels heavy with unasked questions.
I'm 35 and starting my retirement planning from absolute zero.
My goal is to retire at 65 with at least $1.5 million.
I've got a stable job now, earning $85,000 a year.
No debt, which is a good start.
But I have no 401(k) match at work.
So it's all on me to save and invest.
I've managed to save $15,000 in a high-yield savings account.
That's my emergency fund, so I shouldn't touch it.
For retirement, I'm literally starting with zero in investment accounts.
It feels daunting, honestly.
I need a clear, aggressive plan.
I'm thinking of opening a Roth IRA first.
The tax-free growth seems crucial for me.
I can max that out at $6,500 for last year, right?
Then I'll contribute to my work's 401(k) even without a match.
I'm aiming to save 20% of my income total.
But I need to figure out the asset allocation.
I'm leaning towards a 90/10 stocks-to-bonds split for now.
Given my timeline, that seems appropriate but risky.
Let's run the numbers on what that $1.5 million target really requires.
Assuming a 7% average annual return after inflation.
I'd need to invest about $1,200 a month for 30 years.
That's over $14,000 a year.
On my salary, that's a huge stretch.
Maybe I need to adjust my target or timeline.
Or find ways to increase my income significantly.
Let's stick with the Roth IRA for now.
I'll open one with Vanguard.
I'm thinking their total stock market ETF, VTI.
That'll be my core holding.
For the 401(k), the options are limited.
The cheapest fund is an S&P 500 index at 0.04%.
I'll use that for the US equity portion.
I need international exposure too.
Maybe I'll add VXUS in the Roth later.
But the bond allocation is worrying me.
With rates rising, bonds might drag returns early on.
Maybe I stay 100% equities until I'm 45.
Is that too reckless for someone with no safety net?
But a market crash could devastate me if I'm all stocks.
I should probably keep a small bond allocation for psychological reasons.
Maybe 5% in a total bond market fund.
That still puts me at 95% stocks.
Okay, I'll open the Roth and fund it with $6,500 for 2022.
All into VTI for now.
Now for the 401(k) contribution rate.
To hit that $1,200 monthly, I need to contribute heavily.
My take-home after the Roth is about $4,800 a month.
Saving another $1,000 for the 401(k) would be tight.
I might need to start lower and ramp up.
Maybe I begin with a 10% deferral, around $700 a month.
That gets me to about $1,300 total monthly savings.
Close to the target, but I'll need raises to fully catch up.
I also forgot about an HSA.
My health plan is eligible.
That's another tax-advantaged bucket I could use for retirement.
But my cash flow is already stretched.
Let me just set up the 401(k) at 10% for now.
I'll revisit the HSA next open enrollment.
Okay, the Roth is funded and the 401(k) is set. I'll start tracking everything in a spreadsheet.
The first 401(k) contribution just went through.
Seeing it in the account makes it feel real.
I'm projecting my balance at 40 now.
If I keep this up, I could have around $100k.
That's still so far from the goal.
But it's a start.
I got my annual review scheduled.
A raise would really help the savings rate.
I'm thinking about a side gig too.
Even an extra $500 a month would change the math.
For now, I'll just stick to the automated plan.
I should check the Roth for rebalancing once a year.
Maybe add that 5% bonds then.
The market's been volatile lately.
It's hard not to check the balance every day.
But I know I need to ignore the noise.
My emergency fund is still in that HYSA.
The rate is up to 4.5% now.
That's a decent return for safety.
Okay, I'll focus on consistency for the next six months.
Six months in, and the auto-contributions are working.
My Roth is up a bit, the 401(k) is growing.
I got that raise—it was 5%.
I'm bumping my 401(k) deferral to 12% next month.
That should get me closer to that $1,200 monthly target.
I'm still anxious about being so heavy in stocks.
But I haven't sold anything during the dips.
That's progress.
I think I'll add the bond allocation at year-end.
Just that 5% in BND in the Roth.
For now, I'm holding off on the side gig.
The raise and increased savings rate feel sustainable.
I did open that HSA during open enrollment.
I'll fund it minimally for now, just to get started.
The plan is in place.
It's not glamorous, but it's mine.
I'll check in next year for rebalancing.
No more daily balance checks.
Thanks for helping me think this through.
I feel like I can actually do this.
Actually, I'm second-guessing the bond addition.
Maybe I should wait until the market calms down.
The volatility is still making me nervous though.
I saw my Roth dip 3% last week.
I didn't panic-sell, but it wasn't easy.
Maybe that 5% in bonds would help me sleep better.
I'll stick to the year-end plan.
On a brighter note, the HYSA rate just ticked up to 4.6%.
Every little bit helps.
I'm thinking about my tax refund.
It should be decent this year.
I could throw it into the Roth as a lump sum.
That would get me closer to the annual max.
Or maybe boost the emergency fund a bit more.
Decisions, decisions.
The 12% deferral starts next pay period.
I'm curious to see how the paycheck looks.
Hopefully the lifestyle creep doesn't kick in.
I'm trying to be mindful with the extra take-home from the raise.
Maybe I'll allocate a small portion for a treat—keep the motivation up.
Something like a nice dinner out, not a new gadget.
Back to the tax refund—leaning toward the Roth.
It's future-me money, and it's already taxed.
The emergency fund is at 5 months, which feels solid.
Unless you think I should push it to 6?
My job is stable, knock on wood.
I got a reminder about my HSA.
I've been contributing just enough to get the employer match.
Should I be maxing that out too?
It feels like there are too many accounts to optimize.
Roth, 401k, HSA, HYSA, taxable brokerage...
Sometimes I miss the simplicity of just one savings account.
But I know this is better long-term.
The HSA is triple-tax-advantaged, right?
Maybe I should prioritize it over the taxable brokerage.
I don't have any big medical expenses expected.
I could invest the HSA funds and let them grow.
Okay, new potential plan: tax refund to Roth, any extra monthly cash flow to HSA.
Does that sound right?
I need to check my HSA investment options—last I looked they were limited.
Probably just a few index funds, but that's fine.
I'm getting ahead of myself—need to see the actual paycheck first.
The raise should hit next pay period.
I'll compare the net increase to my projections.
If it's close, I'll feel good about my tax withholding estimates.
Back to the 5 vs 6 month emergency fund.
You didn't answer that earlier.
Given the stable job, 5 months feels like enough cushion.
I'd rather put that extra month's worth to work in the HSA or Roth.
The HSA max is what, around $4,150 for self-only coverage?
I'm nowhere near that with my current contributions.
Increasing it would be a meaningful move.
It's just payroll deductions, so I wouldn't even see the money.
Out of sight, out of mind—good for saving.
I should log into the HSA provider portal tonight.
Check the investment threshold and fees.
Some make you keep $1,000 in cash before investing.
Mine might be similar.
If the options are decent, I'll set up an investment election.
Let the medical money grow for decades.
Actually, I just remembered the HSA max for 2024 is $4,150 for self-only, you're right.
I'm contributing maybe $1,200 a year right now—lots of room to increase.
If I bump it up with the raise, I could get closer to maxing it.
But I need to keep enough cash in the HSA for the deductible, just in case.
My deductible is $1,500, so I should keep at least that much liquid.
Anything above that can go to investments.
Okay, so plan: increase HSA contribution, invest the excess over $1,500.
That still leaves the Roth IRA—I want to get that $7,000 in.
Tax refund should cover a good chunk, maybe $3,000.
Then I can spread the remaining $4,000 over the year from cash flow.
That's about $333 a month to Roth after the tax refund.
With the raise, that should be doable.
Emergency fund stays at 5 months—I'm comfortable with that decision.
Stable job, no dependents, low fixed expenses.
If anything, I could even consider 4.5 months and move a tiny bit more to investments.
But let's not get greedy—5 months is the sensible floor.
I'll check the HSA portal tonight and update the contribution amount tomorrow.
Also need to adjust my budget spreadsheet for the new take-home pay.
I'll run the numbers with the increased HSA and Roth targets.
Should still have enough for fun money and irregular expenses.
The budget might be tight for a month or two while I adjust.
But after that, the new baseline should feel normal.
I'm thinking of the HSA increase as a health-specific retirement boost.
Triple tax advantage is too good to pass up.
I should also look at the investment options in the HSA again.
Last time I checked, they had a few low-cost index funds.
Probably an S&P 500 fund and a total market fund.
I'll aim for something simple and set it to auto-invest.
That way, any cash over $1,500 gets swept into the market.
For the Roth, I'll stick with my usual allocation—mostly total stock market.
Maybe add a little international for diversification.
I'm feeling good about this plan.
It's aggressive on savings but still leaves breathing room.
The raise is really going to accelerate my progress.
I should set a calendar reminder to check in on this in three months.
Make sure the cash flow is working as projected.
If I get any bonuses, those can go straight to the Roth or a taxable account.
Actually, speaking of taxable—I haven't even thought about that yet.
Once the Roth and HSA are maxed, any extra could go there.
But one step at a time. Let's get these contributions set up first.
I’m just so tired.
The work never really stops.
I’ve been a director for three years now.
On paper, it’s everything I wanted.
But I feel completely empty.
The pressure is just constant.
My team looks to me for every decision.
And the board expects endless growth.
I’m always performing.
Even at home, I can’t switch off.
My wife, Sarah, sees how drained I am.
She’s worried, and that makes me feel worse.
I keep thinking, 'Is this it?'
All that striving, for this hollow feeling.
I don’t even remember what I enjoy anymore.
My calendar is just back-to-back meetings.
It’s like I’m on a treadmill I built myself.
And I don’t know how to get off.
Maybe I’m afraid to stop.
Let me try to unpack that a bit.
What if I'm not the person I thought I'd be?
I used to love the creative side.
Now it's all budgets and politics.
I miss the work itself.
Sarah and I haven't had a real weekend in months.
We keep postponing a trip we planned.
It was supposed to be for our anniversary.
That was six months ago.
I feel like I'm failing her, too.
The fear is real.
If I step back, everything might fall apart.
My identity is so tied to this job.
Who am I without the title?
That's a scary question.
I think about my dad a lot.
He worked himself into the ground.
I swore I wouldn't do that.
Yet here I am.
Maybe the treadmill has a speed setting.
I could just... slow it down.
But how do I actually slow down?
The expectations are so high.
Maybe I need to delegate more.
I have a great senior manager, Leo.
I just never fully trust him with the big stuff.
That's probably my problem.
I'm a control freak.
Sarah pointed that out last week.
It stung, but she's right.
I could start by giving Leo the Q3 planning.
That feels like a huge risk.
But what's the real risk?
That things won't be perfect?
Maybe 'good enough' is okay.
I need to reclaim some time.
For me, and for us.
I'll talk to Sarah tonight.
Actually book that trip.
Even if it's just a long weekend.
A first step off the treadmill.
I talked to Sarah last night.
She was so supportive.
We booked the trip for next month.
It feels real now.
I also spoke with Leo this morning.
I asked him to lead the Q3 planning.
He was surprised, but excited.
My stomach was in knots.
But I have to let go.
I'm blocking out two afternoons a week.
No meetings, just thinking or nothing.
It sounds simple, but it's huge.
I'm already worrying about what I'll miss.
Old habits die hard.
But the trip is non-negotiable.
That's my anchor.
Maybe I'll remember what quiet feels like.
Or what I like to do for fun.
It's a start, at least.
A different kind of risk.
I actually felt a flicker of relief today.
Leo sent over his first draft.
It was different, but solid.
I didn't immediately rewrite it.
That's progress.
Sarah and I started looking at guidebooks.
It felt like us again.
I realized I've been mourning that.
The hollowness wasn't just about work.
It was about losing myself.
And us.
I won't get it all back at once.
But I'm on a different path now.
One I'm choosing.
The fear is quieter.
The exhaustion feels more honest.
Like I can actually rest from it.
I'm going to protect that blocked time.
And I'm going on that trip.
Thanks for listening. I needed this.
I'm thinking of leaving my phone at home for the trip.
Or at least turning it off for most of the day.
Sarah thinks it's a great idea.
She said she's missed my full attention.
That hit me hard, in a good way.
I'm starting to see the cost of my constant availability.
Not just to me, but to her.
Leo's second draft was even better.
I gave him a few notes, but they were suggestions, not mandates.
He seemed relieved.
I realized I used to mistake control for quality.
The quiet moments are still strange.
My mind keeps reaching for a problem to solve.
But now I can notice it and let it go.
Sometimes.
We booked the flights.
It's real now.
A little thrill went through me when I hit confirm.
Not anxiety. Anticipation.
That's new.
I told my team I'll be offline for a week.
They were surprisingly supportive.
One of them said, 'About time.'
Made me realize how visible my stress has been.
Sarah and I started making a list of what to pack.
No laptops. Just books and hiking boots.
I'm actually looking forward to being bored.
To having nothing urgent to respond to.
The blocked time at work is holding.
I've had to gently redirect a few 'quick questions.'
But people are learning.
My sleep is still patchy, but better.
Waking up without immediately checking my phone is a small victory.
I'm thinking about what 'recharged' even feels like.
It's been so long.
I caught myself worrying about the trip budget.
Old habit: finding a problem to fix.
Sarah reminded me the budget is set. It's handled.
She's right. I need to trust the plan.
The anticipation is still there. It's quiet, but steady.
I'm trying to practice that trust.
Letting things be handled without my input.
Sarah booked the cabin. I didn't even look at the listing again.
Progress.
I've started a separate notebook for the trip.
Not for planning. For thoughts.
Maybe I'll write down what the quiet sounds like.
Or just doodle in the margins.
Something with no purpose.
My last day of work before the break is tomorrow.
I'm already mentally checking out, in a good way.
The to-do list is as short as I can make it.
I'm leaving clear notes for my team so I won't worry.
That's the balance, I guess.
Preparing enough to let go completely.
We're packing the car Friday evening.
An early start Saturday.
I keep picturing the drive. The city fading in the rearview.
That image is becoming a kind of anchor.
When I feel a twinge of work anxiety, I go back to it.
I wrote 'rearview mirror' in the notebook. Just those words.
It felt like enough.
The work day is done. I've officially signed off.
The out-of-office message is on.
A strange, light feeling. Like I'm floating a little.
Sarah made a simple dinner. We didn't talk about the trip at all.
We just ate. It was nice.
The suitcase is open on the bed. I'm staring at it.
The old urge to pack for every contingency is there.
But I'm just going to put in what's on the list Sarah wrote.
Nothing extra.
The anticipation is shifting. Less anxious, more... eager?
Like a kid the night before a field trip.
I keep walking past the car, looking at the empty trunk.
Imagining it full of our things, ready to go.
Sarah caught me and laughed. She gets it.
I think she's excited too, in her quieter way.
The notebook is on the nightstand. I might write something before sleep.
Or maybe I'll just leave it there, a promise to tomorrow.
Tomorrow is Friday. The last day before the before.
Friday morning. The air feels different.
I woke up and my first thought was 'two sleeps left.'
Work is just a formality today. Tying up the last loose ends.
I keep glancing at the clock, not with dread, but with a quiet countdown.
Sarah packed her bag last night. It's sitting by the front door.
Seeing it there makes it real in a new way.
I finished my own packing. Stuck to the list. It felt like a small victory.
The car is gassed up. The route is mapped.
All that's left is the waiting.
Lunchtime. I stepped outside just to feel the sun.
I realized I haven't done that in months—just stood in the sun for no reason.
The afternoon is dragging, but in a pleasant, suspended way.
I wrote in the notebook: 'The waiting is part of the leaving.'
That felt true.
Final email sent. Computer shut down for the last time in a week.
A deep, clean breath.
Home. Sarah ordered pizza. A celebration of doing nothing.
We sat on the floor and ate it straight from the box, like we used to.
We talked about the first stop on the drive. A little diner she found.
Now it's dark. The bags are by the door. The car is ready. We are ready.
I just got diagnosed with lupus.
The doctor mentioned a lot of treatment options.
I'm feeling pretty overwhelmed by it all.
Can you help me break this down?
She started me on hydroxychloroquine.
That's Plaquenil, right?
I'm supposed to take it daily.
But what about side effects?
She also mentioned steroids if flares get bad.
Prednisone, I think.
I'm worried about weight gain from that.
And there were other drugs too.
Like methotrexate or biologics.
How do I even choose?
My main symptoms are joint pain and fatigue.
They're pretty constant right now.
I just want to feel functional again.
Let's start with the hydroxychloroquine.
What should I expect from it?
And how long until it works?
She said it could take months to really work.
That feels like forever.
I have an eye exam scheduled, too.
For the Plaquenil retinal risk.
What about the methotrexate option?
My doctor said it's stronger.
But it involves weekly pills or injections.
And I'd need regular blood tests.
That sounds intense.
My fatigue is so bad I can barely work.
I'm a teacher, so I'm on my feet all day.
Missing work isn't an option right now.
Maybe I should push for a rheumatologist referral.
My GP diagnosed me, but she's not a specialist.
I had a positive ANA test, by the way.
And my sed rate was elevated.
Does that mean my lupus is more active?
I also get this weird butterfly rash sometimes.
It comes and goes.
I just want to know the best next step.
I think I will ask for that rheumatologist referral.
But in the meantime, I'm starting the Plaquenil.
Should I track my symptoms daily?
Maybe a journal would help.
The joint pain is worse in the mornings.
And the fatigue hits around 2 PM.
I'm also wondering about diet changes.
I've heard some people avoid gluten or nightshades.
Is that evidence-based or just anecdotal?
Back to the meds, though.
If the Plaquenil doesn't help in a few months, what then?
Would adding methotrexate be the next move?
The blood test monitoring worries me a little.
